title: 160 years of oil prices — and every crisis that moved them
subtitle: Crude oil prices, $2014/barrel, 1861-2026
source: Bloomberg, BP, Federal Reserve, Haver Analytics, Goldman Sachs GIR
editorialPoint: The 2026 Hormuz crisis has driven oil prices higher faster than any previous shock — but the world is also less energy-intensive than during any previous crisis

annotations.label: US Civil War drives up commodity prices
Post-war auto boom creates fuel shortages
Arab oil embargo after Yom Kippur War
Demand surge, speculation, Middle East tensions
Hormuz closure — largest supply shock in history

note: The 2026 crisis represents ~20% of global supply disrupted — 3-5x larger than any previous shock. But oil intensity per dollar of GDP has halved since 1973, meaning the GDP drag per dollar of price increase is smaller than in historical precedents.
